coredata.dc:title: Photo-sonodynamic combination activity of cationic morpholino-phthalocyanines conjugated to nitrogen and nitrogen-sulfur doped graphene quantum dots against MCF-7 breast cancer cell line in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Nene, Lindokuhle Cindy
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we explore the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation abilities of cationic morpholino-substituted-phthalocyanine (Pc) conjugated to nitrogen (NGQDs) and nitrogen-sulfur (NSGQDs) doped-graphene quantum dots upon irradiation with light for photodynamic therapy (PDT), ultrasound for sonodynamic therapy (SDT) and the combination of both in photo-sonodynamic therapy (PSDT). The in vitro cytotoxicity studies were conducted using the Michigan Cancer Foundation-7 breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7 cells). For PDT treatments, only the 1O2 was detected for all the sensitizers, whereas both the 1O2 and •OH radicals were evident after SDT and PSDT treatments. An increase in the 1O2 generation was observed for the conjugates compared to the GQDs and the Pc alone. However, the •OH radicals were reduced in the conjugates compared to the GQDs and the Pc alone. The NGQDs generally showed better ROS generation efficacy compared to the NSGQDs, alone and in the conjugates. The combination therapy also shows improved efficacy compared to the monotherapies for the Pcs and Pc-GQDs conjugates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cancer
Photodynamic therapy
Sonodynamic therapy
Phthalocyanines
Graphene quantum dots
Singlet oxygen
Hydroxyl radicals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Pc-GQDS conjugates
Singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radical generation and measurements
Cell culture for PDT, SDT and PSDT studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Republic of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of Pc-NGQDs and Pc-NSGQDs conjugates.
EDS spectra of the Pc, GQDs and the conjugates.
Estimation of the GQDs and conjugate sizes using DLS size in water.
Raman spectra of the GQDs and the conjugates in water.

Change in absorption intensities of the Pc and conjugates before and after light projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2) and ultrasound (Frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles) irradiation for 10 min in water. US = Ultrasound.
UV–vis. absorption, emission, and excitation spectra in water.

Reactive oxygen species generation in water after light (projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2) or ultrasound (frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles) irradiation for 10 min, monitored by EPR using 50 mM each of TEMP (for singlet oxygen) or DMPO (for hydroxide radicals) and 25 µg/mL of each sensitizer. US: Ultrasound.

Reactive oxygen species generation in water after light (projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2) and ultrasound (frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles) combination irradiation for 10 min, monitored by EPR using 50 mM of TEMP (for singlet oxygen) or DMPO (for hydroxide radicals) and 25 µg/mL of each sensitizer. US = Ultrasound.

Change in cellular morphology 24 h after PSDT treatment with 40 µg/mL of the Pc, NGQDs, NSGQDs, Pc-NGQDs and Pc-NSGQDs. Parameters for PDT = irradiation doses of 170 J.cm−2, power 2.3 W at 680 nm. SDT: frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles, PDT applied first. Control samples: (A) non-treated cells, (B) treated with US alone, and (C) treated with the combination of US and light, all without the Pc, GQDs or conjugates.
WST-assay for sensitizer toxicity at varied concentrations without light nor ultrasound irradiations in water. n = 3.

WST-assay post- PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments at varied concentrations in water at 10 min irradiation time, n = 3. Parameters: PDT = irradiation doses of 170 J.cm−2, power 2.3 W at 680 nm. SDT: frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles.PSDT = combined PDT and SDT parameters with PDT applied first.

Cellular sensitizer-uptake after 24 h incubation with the sensitizers at the varied test concentrations. Intensities = a.u. x 10−1. n = 3. Excitation at 610 nm for the Pc and conjugates and 333 nm for GQDs alone.
Summary of the structural and photophysical properties in water. Values in brackets are for excitations where the GQDs absorb.

row.entry:
Loading
(mmol of Pc/ mg of conjugate)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of EPR signal intensities (a.u. × 103). US = ultrasound. Parameters: Light = (projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2), ultrasound (frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles).
Summary of the IC50 for the treatments in μg/mL. n = 3.

head.ce:title: Photo-sonodynamic combination activity of cationic morpholino-phthalocyanines conjugated to nitrogen and nitrogen-sulfur doped graphene quantum dots against MCF-7 breast cancer cell line in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, P.O. 94, Makhanda, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, P.O. 94, Makhanda, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we explore the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation abilities of cationic morpholino-substituted-phthalocyanine (Pc) conjugated to nitrogen (NGQDs) and nitrogen-sulfur (NSGQDs) doped-graphene quantum dots upon irradiation with light for photodynamic therapy (PDT), ultrasound for sonodynamic therapy (SDT) and the combination of both in photo-sonodynamic therapy (PSDT). The in vitro cytotoxicity studies were conducted using the Michigan Cancer Foundation-7 breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7 cells). For PDT treatments, only the 1O2 was detected for all the sensitizers, whereas both the 1O2 and •OH radicals were evident after SDT and PSDT treatments. An increase in the 1O2 generation was observed for the conjugates compared to the GQDs and the Pc alone. However, the •OH radicals were reduced in the conjugates compared to the GQDs and the Pc alone. The NGQDs generally showed better ROS generation efficacy compared to the NSGQDs, alone and in the conjugates. The combination therapy also shows improved efficacy compared to the monotherapies for the Pcs and Pc-GQDs conjugates.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been studied and developed for effective cancer therapy as it provides a non-invasive, cancer-specific and a controllable therapeutic approach [1,2]. This treatment technique involves the synergistic activity of non-cytotoxic chemical compounds (known as photosensitizers) with light of specific wavelength and molecular oxygen (O2). During treatment, the photosensitizers are irradiated with light, causing them to be excited and occupy higher energy levels. The excited sensitizer thereafter transfers energy to the O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as singlet oxygen (1O2) [2,3]. The ROS initiates cellular oxidative-stress which promotes apoptosis and/or necrosis, therefore eradicating the cancer [4–6]. Although PDT is better than the conventional treatment techniques including radiation, chemotherapy, and surgery, it is still limited to the treatment of superficial cancers as the light used can only penetrate up to 10 mm below the epidermis [7], thus, making it not suitable for treating deep tissue-seated tumors [7,8]. To curb this limitation, sonodynamic therapy has been gaining more interest and is being developed as an alternative or supplementary cancer treatment technique to PDT [9,10].

Sonodynamic therapy (SDT), similar to PDT, uses sensitizers (known as sonosensitizers). However, ultrasound is used to activate the sonosensitizers instead of light [11], to generate cytotoxic species in the cellular environment exposed to both the sensitizers and ultrasound [12,13]. The ultrasound mechanical waves have a low attenuation coefficient and may be focused in a narrow/ specific area [14], thus, offering a non-invasive and controllable technique with the opportunity of reaching soft tissue cancers. The exact mechanism of action in SDT has not yet been clearly defined, however it is believed that the ultrasound exerted in the aqueous medium results in the formation of gas-filled bubbles [15–17]. These bubbles grow to resonance size and thereafter burst to emit light known as sonoluminescence. The energy from this light may be absorbed by the sonosensitizers causing them to be excited and result in the ROS generation similarly to PDT [18–20]. The bursting bubbles also release high energy and pressure in the microenvironment and may cause sonosensitizers to fracture to form radicals derived from the sensitizer fragments. The phenomenon of bubble formation and bursting in SDT is known as acoustic cavitation [19]. SDT has been studied as a monotherapy or in combination with PDT, for photo-sonodynamic combination therapy (PSDT) [21–24]. During PSDT, a single sensitizer that can be activated by both ultrasound and light is usually used. In this work, we report phthalocyanine-mediated PSDT.

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are a group of organic dyes which are electron-rich tetra-pyrrolic macrocycles [25,26]. These dyes have been widely studied for applications in PDT. There have been reports on the applications of Pcs in SDT [27–29] and fewer for PSDT [30,31]. The interest in Pcs as sensitizers is owed to their impressive physico-chemical properties such as their intense absorption in the near infrared region of the UV-vis spectrum [32,33]. The Pcs are also stable under physiological conditions, they are structurally tunable to improve efficacy and can generate ROS upon activation by light and/ or ultrasound. The Pcs are however, generally lipophilic and tend to aggregate in aqueous media. The impact of the aggregation on the therapeutic efficacy is seen in the reduction of the ROS generation, both in PDT and SDT [28,34]. In this work we employ Pcs with morpholine substituents on the peripheral positions, which are bulky and may reduce aggregation in aqueous media. The morpholine moieties are well known for use as anticancer agents including in PDT [35–37]. Additionally, the morpholine affords the Pcs tertiary amine-groups which may be methylated to form cationic Pcs and therefore achieve improved hydrophilicity. The cations on the Pcs also improve efficacy by assisting in the targeting and delivery of the sensitizers to the negatively charged cancer membrane through opposite charge attraction. Moreover, cationic chemical compounds are known for enhanced therapeutic efficacy [38–41].

Pcs are usually conjugated to nanomaterials which are used mainly as delivery carriers including inorganic metallic nanoparticles such as gold or silver nanoparticles or organic non-metallic nanoparticles including micelles or various graphene sheets to mention a few [42 , 43]. In this work, we employ graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the potential nanocarriers for the Pcs. The GQDs are highly fluorescent hydrophilic aromatic macro-carbon sheets with a conjugated π-electron rich system. Two types of GQDs were used, the nitrogen doped GQDs (NGQDs) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs (NSGQDs). The Pcs have been loaded on the GQDs’ surfaces through π-π electron interactions between the Pcs and GQDs [44]. The introduction of heteroatoms such as N, and S to carbon materials enhances catalytic activity [45]. Co-doping of carbon nanomaterials with two heteroatoms create more active sites compared to single–doped counterparts [45], hence in this work we employ NS doped GQDs in addition to N doped GQDs. Doping of nanomaterials with heteroatoms (especially N, S doping) has been shown to improve PDT activity of Pcs when the two are conjugated [46]. This is the first time that GQDs and Pcs are used together for SDT or PSDT. The benefit of GQDs is mostly on the fact that the larger compounds are less likely to be taken up by healthy somatic cells due to cellular membrane pore-size differences. Where cancer cells may easily take up larger molecules since they have larger pores on their membrane than healthy cells.

The aim of this work is to prepare the cationic morpholino-phthalocyanines conjugated to NGQDs and NSGQDs by π-π electron interactions, and to study the effect of the GQDs on the PDT, SDT and PSDT activity on breast cancer cell lines, in vitro.
The instruments used in the characterization of the Pc, GQDs and conjugates are summarized in the Supplementary information.

The chemicals and solvents used in this work are reagent grade. The 2,2,6,6-tetra-methylpiperidone (TEMP); 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were all purchased from Sigma Aldrich®, United States of America. Ultra-pure water was obtained from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, flex system, Marlow, United Kingdom. The human breast adenocarcinoma Michigan cancer-7 (MCF-7) cell line were purchased from Cellonex®, South Africa. The Dulbecco phosphate-buffer saline (PBS) and Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®, Switzerland. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10% (v/v)); the 100 μg/ mL-penicillin:100 unit/ mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B-mixture (PSA) and neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST) were all purchased from Biowest®, France. The 2,9(10),16(17),23(24) -tetrakis-(N-methyl-morpholino) zinc (II) phthalocyanines (1) was synthesized as previously reported [47]. The NGQDs and NSGQDs were also prepared using methods reported in the literature [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Pc-GQDS conjugates
Singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radical generation and measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS generation was determined using EPR spectroscopy (instrument details are given in Supplementary information). EPR spin trapping reagents TEMP and DMPO were used to detect the 1O2 and •OH radicals, respectively. Solutions containing 25 μg/mL of each of the test samples (Pc, GQDs and conjugates) in water and a spin trapping reagent (50 mM) were prepared and irradiated with light (projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2) or ultrasound (frequency = 1 MHz, power = 1 W.cm−2, 100% duty cycles) or the combination of both for 10 min. Details of equipment are in the Supporting Information. The formation of radicals was determined by obtaining the EPR spectrum thereafter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture for PDT, SDT and PSDT studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The MCF7-cells were seeded into 96-well plates up to ∼80% confluency (∼10, 000 cells/ well) in culture media (DMEM with 4.5 g/ L glucose with l-glutamine and phenol red, supplemented with 10% FBS and 5% PSA) in a 37 ⁰C, ∼5% CO2 humidified atmosphere incubator from Heat-Force®. The sensitizers were added to the wells at concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 µg/mL. After 24 h incubation, the cells were irradiated with light or ultrasound for 10 min during treatments. For light irradiations, the Modulight® Medical Laser System (MLS) 7710–680 channel Turnkey laser system connected to a 2.3 W channel at 680 nm with irradiation doses of 170 J.cm−2 was used. The irradiation kit has the capacity to hold a 96-well tissue culture plate with dimensions of 127.76 × 85.48 mm. More details maybe found in the Supporting Information. Our group has studied the SDT activity of morpholine-substituted Pc at the following ultrasonication parameters varying the frequency and power of the ultrasound: (i) 1 MHz : 1 W.cm−2; (ii) 1 MHz : 2 W.cm−2; (iii) 3 MHz : 1 W.cm−2 and (iv) 3 MHz : 2 W.cm−2 [38]. When increasing the frequency to 3 MHz, the Pc's 1O2 and •OH generation is reduced. When increasing the power to 2 W.cm−2, the degree of degradation of the Pc is increased. Hence, the parameter combination: 1 MHz: 1 W.cm−2 was selected for SDT in this work since it showed minimal degradation and relatively good ROS generation compared to the other parameter combinations. For PSDT, the same ultrasound and PDT conditions were employed. The ultrasound slightly degraded the Pc as previously reported [38], hence the order of irradiations was light first followed by altrasound. After irradiations, the cells were incubated for 24 h. The cells were thereafter washed with PBS and new culture media was added. A volume of 5 μL of tetrazolium salt WST-1 reagent was added to each well and re-incubated for an additional 5 h. Live cells reduce the tetrazolium salt WST-1 reagent to form the orange formazan. The absorption intensity of the formazan at 450 nm was measured using the Synergy™ 2 multi-mode microplate reader BioTek. The percentage of cell survival for each of treatment sample was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
C e l l
s u r v i v a l =
Abs 450
( treatment )
Abs 450
( control )
× 100 %
where Abs450(treatment) is the absorption intensity at 450 nm for the treatment sample and Abs450(control) is the absorption intensity at 450 nm for the control sample. Control samples including cells treated with the sensitizer alone; light alone, ultrasound alone, light and ultrasound alone and cells not exposed to the sensitizers nor light nor ultrasound were also prepared.

For cellular morphology studies, the cells were grown and treated with the sensitizers and light and/ or ultrasound as detailed in the cytotoxicity studies. The cells were then washed with PBS and 100 μL of clear DMEM solution was added to each well. The cellular images were captured using the Zeiss® AxioVert.A1 Fluorescence LED (FL-LED) inverted microscope connected to a Zeiss® camera.

For cellular uptake, the sensitizers were added to the cells using the same concentrations as in the cytotoxicity studies and then incubated for 24 h. The cells were washed with PBS and thereafter, 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well and left for 1 h. The cells were sonicated for 5 min to dissolve the sensitizers. The fluorescence intensities of the sensitizers in the wells were measured using the SpectraMax® M3, molecular Devices multi-well-plate reader from Separations and recorded using the SoftMax® Pro6.4 software. The fluorescence of the quantum dots alone was measured by exciting them at 333 nm and for Pc and conjugates the excitation wavelength was 610 nm.

The structural properties of the Pc, the GQDs and the Pc -GQDs conjugates were confirmed using various analytical techniques. The syntheses the conjugates of Pc with NGQDs and NSGQDs is shown in Scheme 1 .

The EDS spectra show the elemental compositions of the Pc and GQDs before and after conjugation (Fig. 1 ). From the spectra, the NGQDs show the presence of the main elements, C atoms at ∼0.2 keV, N atoms at ∼0.4 keV, and O atoms at ∼0.6 keV with NSGQDs showing an additional S atom peak at ∼2.4 keV. In the presence of the Pc, Zn atoms are also evident with peaks showing at ∼1 keV, 8.6 keV and 9.6 keV.

The average sizes of the GQDs and conjugates were determined using DLS (Fig. 2 ). The GQDs were found to have an average diameter size of 8.5 and 7.5 nm for NGQDs and NSGQDs, respectively, Table 1
. Upon conjugation to the Pc, the sizes increased up to an average of 18.2 and 21.0 nm for Pc-NGQDs and Pc-NSGQDs respectively, Table 1 . The increase in size was expected for the conjugates as the Pcs molecules stack on top of the GDQs, therefore increasing their overall sizes.

Raman spectroscopy was used to study the quality of the GQDs and conjugates. A typical Raman spectrum for GQDs shows two prominent peaks denoted the G-band (for sp2 hybridized groups) and D band (sp3 hybridized groups) (Fig. 3 ). The change in the intensities and sometimes positions of the G and D bands of the Raman spectra are attributed to the introduction of structural defects on the surface of the GQDs and the stacking of compounds into multiple layers on top of the GQDs [50]. An increase in the D band intensities relative to the G band suggests introduction of surface defects. The degree of defects on the surfaces of the GQDs is calculated by the quotients of the D and G- bands intensities (ID/IG). The ID/IG of the GQDs and conjugates are summarized in Table 1 . The ID/IG NSGQDs is larger than that of the NGQDs, this may be due to the presence of larger S groups on the surfaces, causing surface roughness. The ID/IG further increase for both the GQDs upon conjugation to the Pc. This is expected as the Pc introduces atomic groups causing the overall surface of the conjugates to be rough. Furthermore, the shifting of the D band is due to the stacking of the Pc and is indicative of formation of multiple layers on the conjugates. The significant blue shift of Raman bands of GQDs may be explained by the strain effect caused by the Pc, as reported in literature for other substrates on graphene [51].

The loading of the Pc onto GQDs’ surfaces was estimated using UV-vis spectroscopy using a method previously described in the literature [52]. The loading values are summarized in Table 1 . The loading of the Pc was 1.32 mmol/ mg of Pc-NGQDs and 1.10 mmol/ mg of Pc-NSGQDs.

The stability of the sensitizers under light or ultrasound irradiations were studied (Fig. 4 ) since it is important to consider for the overall efficacy of the sensitizers where degradation of sensitizer during treatment may lead to the formation of non-active fragments. The change in the UV-vis absorption intensities upon exposure to light or ultrasound in water were determined, where a decrease in UV-vis implies structral degradation.

The UV-vis spectrum of the Pc in water shows a maximum absorption peak, denoted as the Q-band at 673 nm, Table 1 . There is a decrease in the Q-band for the Pc alone and a relatively lower decrease in Pc -NGQDs when irradiated with ultrasound and no change observed for Pc-NSGQDs. No degradation was observed after light irradiation for any of the samples. Therefore, the Pc and the conjugates are stable after light irradiation. There is a negligable change in the absorption intensity in the region where GQDs, Fig. 5 . The GQDs on their own showed no decrease in absorption intensity after light Fig. S1. However, the NSGQDs showed a slight decrease after ultrasound irradiation and the NGQDs did not, Fig. S1. For combination studies, the order of irradiation was therefore set as light first followed by ultrasound to minimize the possible effects of sensitizer degradation. A study by Bakhshizadeh et al. showed that the order of irradiation does affect the overall PSDT activity of the Pcs and that light irradiation first and ultrasound afterwards showed a better result compared to the reversed order [31,53].

The UV-vis absorption, emission and excitation spectra are shown in Fig. 5 . Upon conjugation, the Q-band broadens for Pc-NGQDs and Pc-NSGQDs. The broadening of the peaks may be attributed to the formation of aggregates as the Pcs stack on the GQDs surfaces. The Pcs are known for π-π stacking [54]. At the B-band, the intensities increased for the conjugates due to the presence of GQDs which absorb in that region of the UV-vis spectrum, Fig. 5. The emission spectrum of the Pc obtained upon excitation at 610 nm is a mirror-images of the absorption spectrum. This observation is not the case with the conjugates where the emission spectrum is different from the absorption spectrum due to aggregation, Fig. 5.

The ФF for the Pc and Pc-GQDs conjugates were calculated using a comparative method reported in the literature [55]. The unsubstituted Zinc Pc in DMSO (ФF = 0.20) [56] was used as a standard for excitation at 610 nm (where the Pc absorbs) and quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (ФF = 0.54) [57] as a standard for excitation at 333 nm (where the GQDs absorb). Studies on the conjugates when exciting where the GQDs absorb, were not undertaken due to the complication of exciting both the Pc (at the B band) and the GQDs.

The ФF of the Pc was calculated to be 0.16 in water, where a decrease to 0.14 was observed upon conjugation to the Pc-NGQDs and Pc-NSGQDs. This decrease may be attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer between the excited singlet state of the Pc and the GQDs in the conjugates [58]. The fluorescence lifetimes were determined using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique and exciting where the GQDs absorb for the GQDs alone and where the Pcs absorb for the Pc alone and conjugates. The shortening of fluorescence lifetime of the Pc in the conjugate, is also observed, Table 1
, for the reasons give above for fluorescence quantum yields.
The EPR spectroscopy was used to determine the generation of ROS in water Fig. 6 .

The generation of ROS was monitored using 50 mM of the EPR spin trapping reagents (TEMP and DMPO for 1O2 and •OH radical detection, respectively). In the presence of 1O2, a three-peaked signal is observed between 3450 and 3500 G on the EPR spectrum, where a four-peaked signal shows instead in the case where •OH radicals are present ( Figs. 6
, 7 ). For the light monotherapy only 1O2 generation is observed for all samples. For ultrasound monotherapy and light-ultrasound combination therapy, both the 1O2 and •OH radicals were observed ( Figs. 6
, 7). The absence of •OH was expected as light-mediated ROS generation by Pcs is known to occur through the Type II ROS generation route which involves the conversion of O2 to 1O2 [59]. The intensities of the EPR signals for both the 1O2 and •OH radicals are directly proportional to the amounts of radicals formed. Higher ROS generated in the sample is related to higher EPR-peak intensities.

The EPR signal intensities were determined using the first peak for TEMP signals and the second peak for DMPO signals as highlighted Fig. 7 and are summarized in Table 2
.

The preparation of the control samples is summarized in the supplementary information. All controls showed no ROS generation (Fig. S2). Under light irradiation, the GQDs alone showed the least 1O2 generation compared to the Pc and the conjugates. The NGQDs however, showed better performance compared to the NSGQDs ( Table 2 ). This observation may be attributed to the fact that the NGQDs have a relatively lower ФF and allowing more energy to be used in the generation of 1O2 as opposed to it being lost through fluorescence. The 1O2 generation of the Pc increased upon conjugation with the GQDs during light irradiation. The improvement of 1O2 generation has been reported before in the literture for the S-containing GQDs [60]. The Pc-NGQDs showed better performance compared to the Pc-NSGQDs. This may be due to the NGQDs alone generating relatively higher ROS than NSGQDs, Table 2 , hence increasing ROS generation by synergestic effect. In addition, Fig. 4 shows aggregation in the conjugates, which quenches ROS generation [61] to a differing extend in the two conjugates. Overall, the 1O2 generation increased for the Pc upon conjugation with the GQDs under irradiations with light, ultrasound and the combination of both. The •OH radicals were drastically reduced instead. Interestingly, the NGQDs and the Pc showed high •OH radical generation under ultrasound and combination of ultrasound and light. However, for the Pc-NGQDs relatively lower •OH yields were observed. In general, NGQDs perform better with the 1O2 and •OH generation compared to the NSGQDs. The combination of light and ultrasound also showed an enhancement in the generation of both the 1O2 and •OH for all the samples compared to samples irradiated with light or ultrasound alone ( Table 2 ). Therefore, the combination therapy is better than the monotherapies. Moreover, the presence of GQDs in the conjugates still play a vital role as it offers the benefit of being vectors for the Pcs for therapy.

The effect of the PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments mediated by the Pc, the GQDs and conjugates, on the morphology of the cells was monitored using microscopic imaging (Fig. 8 ). Healthy MCF-7 cells have an eye-like shape and are surface-adherent [62]. Upon treatment, the overall cell-size is reduced, the cells constrict, forming circular-shapes. The cells ultimately die, and they lose their surface adherence property causing them to float in the media solution [63]. From Fig. 8, the control samples including untreated cells; those exposed to ultrasound alone or light and ultrasound combined, showed healthy cells. Additional controls included cells irradiated with light alone and those only exposed to the sensitizers alone. Each of the control samples showed little to no changes in the cellular morphologies compared to the healthy untreated cells. The application of light or ultrasound with the Pc, the GQDs and conjugates in the PDT, SDT as well PSDT (only PSDT is shown as an example in Fig. 8), resulted in a change in the morphology of the cells because of cell destruction imposed by the treatment techniques (Fig. 8).

The cells treated with the Pc showed morphological change and appeared circular in shape. The cells also lost their surface-adherence properties. The clustering of cells and clear spaces indicate that most cells have lifted and were floating in the media. The cell death was also confirmed by observation of empty patches on the culture flasks as cells were no longer adherent and are removed with the media when washing. Cells treated with just the NGQDs and NSGQDs also showed cells which have not yet lifted. Cells treated with the Pc-NGQDs compared to those treated with Pc-NSGQDs, had mostly lifted.

The therapeutic efficacy of the PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments was monitored by the quantification of surviving cells post-treatment. The cells were quantified using the WST-1 cell proliferation assay ( Figs. 9
and 10 ). The cell-survival percentage of the sensitizer-only control samples were determined to confirm the non-toxicity of the sensitizers without activation by light or ultrasound. The MCF7-cells were exposed to the sensitizers at varied test concentrations and incubated in the dark for 24 h. The cells were washed with PBS and new culture media was thereafter added. The cells were then re-incubated in the dark. The percentages of live cells were calculated relative to untreated cell samples (0 μg/mL concentration of sensitizer and no exposure to light or ultrasound) using the WST-1 cell proliferation assay.

The sensitizers showed minimal cellular toxicity with increasing concentrations Fig. 9, all showing cell survival above 80%. This also supports that the Pc, GQDs or conjugates alone are not suitable for eradicating cancers unless exposed to an activating-stimuli such as light or ultrasound. Cell survival post-treatment with the combination of sensitizer with light or ultrasound was determined, Fig. 10.

All the sensitizers demonstrated cytotoxicity for the PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments (Fig. 10). An increase in the treatment efficacy was observed with an increase in the concentrations of the sensitizers. A decrease in cell survival after treatment with the sensitizers for all three therapies was observed (Fig. 10). Complementary to the results as seen with the EPR spectra, all sensitizers were able to eradicate the cancer cells at varying degrees of efficacy. This effect involves inducing oxidative stress in the cellular environment using the generated ROS. The cell survival percentages at the highest concentrations used (50 μg/mL) are summarized in Table S1 and a summary of the IC50 values is shown in Table 3
.

The overall PDT, SDT and PSDT of the Pc alone was relatively better compared to the conjugates and the GQDs alone ( Table 3 ). The conjugates did, however, show impressive efficiency in eradicating the cancer cells, even though relatively less efficiently than the Pc alone. The NGQDs and NSGQDs alone also showed the ability to eradicate cancer cells upon irradiation with light or ultrasound or both, with the NGQDs showing better performance than the NSGQDs, Fig. 10. However, both the GQDs had lower therapeutic efficacies for all treatment types compared to the Pc alone and the conjugates. These observations are complementary to those seen with the ROS generation previously. Therefore, although the GQDs generate ROS upon light or ultrasound, they alone are not effective sensitizers compared to the Pcs and Pc-GQDs conjugates. An increase in the effectivity of the treatments was observed for all sensitizers in the PSDT combination therapy compared to the PDT and SDT monotherapies as seen with the reduction in the cell survival percentages (Fig. 10 , Table S1). The Pc alone performed better for SDT, PDT and PSDT compared to the conjugates, even though the conjugates gave higher 1O2 production compared to the Pc under light or ultrasound irradiations and the combination of both. The Pc did however show higher production of the •OH radicals under both ultrasound and combined irradiations. Thus, it is likely that the •OH radicals also play a significant role in the eradication of cancer, hence the Pc with the higher •OH generating ability, in addition to reasonable 1O2 generation would perform better. As stated above positively charged complexes show enhanced activity, the presence of GQDs could have neutralized the charge on the Pc, hence reduced activity. Thus, the current study shows that it is not always the 1O2 which determines the PDT activity.

The IC50 values were extrapolated from the cell survival data to determine the concentration of the sensitizers required to eradicate ∼50% of the cells for each treatment technique. These values are summarized in Table 3 . The IC50 value for the Pc was relatively lower than that the conjugates for all three therapies. This is expected as the Pc showed slightly higher efficacy than the conjugates at the same mass/volume concentrations. For the Pc alone and the conjugates, the IC50 values were slightly better in SDT treatments than the PDT. This observation may be expected since the SDT technique uses both type I and type II ROS generation route which allows for the formation of both the 1O2 and •OH radicals generation. For PDT, however, only the type II route is possible, as seen with the absence of •OH radicals previously with EPR (Fig. 6). The PSDT treatments were overall relatively more efficient than both the monotherapies.

The cancer cells have a negative charge potential due to the overexpression of lactic acids and other anionic lipids in the cells [64]. Therefore, the Pcs, including conjugates, are expected to be retained by the cells. Studies have shown that the cationic Pcs are better PDT performers compared to their neutral counterparts due to the improved cellular uptake, causing an increase in the availability of sensitizer required for treatment [62,65,66].

From Fig. 11, a more linear increase in sensitizer quantity in the cells is seen with the Pc, where the fluorescence increases steadily from 0 to ∼40 μg/mL, and at concentrations above 40 μg/mL, the sensitizer concentrations begin to plateau. This may be observed since the Pc is a relatively smaller molecule compared to the conjugates and may be taken up to larger concentrations before the cells are saturated. The GQDs are also well retained by the cells through passive uptake since the cancer cells characteristically have a leaky membrane structure with large pores. There is a sharper increase in concentrations from 0 to ∼20 μg/mL for the GQDs and conjugates compared to the Pc alone. The cellular concentrations also began to plateau at ∼40 μg/mL for the NGQDs. The NSGQDs and conjugates showing slower cellular concentration increases from ∼20/ 30 μg/mL. The relatively quicker uptake seen with the GQDs and conjugates at lower concentrations and slower uptake at higher concentrations may be due to the sizes. The bigger molecules may saturate much quicker in the cells than the smaller molecules. In addition, the GQDs alone were excited at a different wavelength from the Pc, and they are highly fluorescent and will therefore have a relatively higher fluorescence compared to the Pcs at the same mass concentrations.

The NGQDs and NSGQDs conjugates with a morpholino-substituted Pcs were prepared and their PDT, SDT and combination therapeutic (PSDT) activities were studied. The Pc, GQDs and conjugates showed 1O2 and •OH radical formation when monitored using EPR for PSDT and SDT techniques. For PDT, only 1O2 was generated. The 1O2 generation increased upon conjugation, but the opposite for •OH radicals which decreased for the Pc when it was conjugated to the GQDS. Moreover, the combination of light and ultrasound increased the activity of the sensitizers. In the therapy studies, a low cellular survival percentage was observed for samples treated with the Pc, using light, ultrasound or both ultrasound and light compared to GQDs alone or the conjugates. No, or minimal cytotoxicity was observed for samples treated with the Pc, GQDs and conjugates alone (with no US, light irradiations), therefore showing that the sensitizers are not cytotoxic and can only exhibit lethal effects when activated by light or ultrasound.

Lindokuhle Cindy Nene: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Tebello Nyokong: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2021.102573.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The story of MCF-7 breast cancer ceall line: 40 years of experience in research
